title: Jules Bastien-Lepage

infobox.value: (1 November 1848 – 10 December 1884) was a French [painter](/wiki/Painting "Painting") closely associated with the beginning of [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(visual_art) "Naturalism (visual art)"), an artistic style that grew out of the [Realist movement](/wiki/Realism_(art_movement) "Realism (art movement)") and paved the way for the development of [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"). [Émile Zola](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Zola "Émile Zola") described Bastien-Lepage's work as "impressionism corrected, sweetened and adapted to the taste of the crowd."[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French painter (1848–1884)

| Jules Bastien-Lepage | |
| --- | --- |
| *Self-Portrait* | |
| Born | (1848-11-01)1 November 1848  [Damvillers](/wiki/Damvillers "Damvillers"), [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse_(department) "Meuse (department)"), France |
| Died | 10 December 1884(1884-12-10) (aged 36)  Paris, France |
| Education | [École des beaux-arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_nationale_sup%C3%A9rieure_des_Beaux-Arts "École nationale supérieure des Beaux-Arts") |
| Known for | [Painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting") |
| Movement | [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(visual_art) "Naturalism (visual art)") |
|  | |
| Signature | |

|  | |

**Jules Bastien-Lepage** (1 November 1848 – 10 December 1884) was a French [painter](/wiki/Painting "Painting") closely associated with the beginning of [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(visual_art) "Naturalism (visual art)"), an artistic style that grew out of the [Realist movement](/wiki/Realism_(art_movement) "Realism (art movement)") and paved the way for the development of [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"). [Émile Zola](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Zola "Émile Zola") described Bastien-Lepage's work as "impressionism corrected, sweetened and adapted to the taste of the crowd."[[1]](#cite_note-1)

His *[en plein air](/wiki/En_plein_air "En plein air")* depictions of peasant life in the countryside were highly influential on many international artists, including [George Clausen](/wiki/George_Clausen "George Clausen") in England and [Tom Roberts](/wiki/Tom_Roberts "Tom Roberts") in Australia. He also won renown for his history paintings, among the most famous being *[Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc_(painting) "Joan of Arc (painting)")*, now held at the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in
[New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Life and work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and work")]

Bastien-Lepage was born in the village of [Damvillers](/wiki/Damvillers "Damvillers"), [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse_(department) "Meuse (department)"), and spent his childhood there.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrantz1911-3) Bastien's father grew grapes in a vineyard to support the family. His grandfather also lived in the village; his garden had [espaliered](/wiki/Espalier "Espalier") fruit trees of apple, pear, and peach up against the high walls. Bastien took an early liking to drawing, and his parents fostered his creativity by buying prints of paintings for him to copy.

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Rodin_-_Jules_Bastien-Lepage_CAC_front.JPG/250px-Rodin_-_Jules_Bastien-Lepage_CAC_front.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rodin_-_Jules_Bastien-Lepage_CAC_front.JPG)

[Auguste Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin"), *Jules Bastien-Lepage*, 1887, on display at the [Iris & B. Gerald Cantor Center for Visual Arts](/wiki/Iris_%26_B._Gerald_Cantor_Center_for_Visual_Arts "Iris & B. Gerald Cantor Center for Visual Arts") on the [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") campus in [Stanford, California](/wiki/Stanford,_California "Stanford, California")

Jules Bastien-Lepage's first teacher was his father, himself an artist.[[4]](#cite_note-cassell-4) His first formal training was at [Verdun](/wiki/Verdun-sur-Meuse "Verdun-sur-Meuse"). Prompted by a love of art, he went to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") in 1867, where he was admitted to the [École des Beaux-arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_nationale_sup%C3%A9rieure_des_Beaux-Arts "École nationale supérieure des Beaux-Arts"), working under [Alexandre Cabanel](/wiki/Alexandre_Cabanel "Alexandre Cabanel").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrantz1911-3) He was awarded first place for drawing, but spent most of his time working alone, only occasionally appearing in class. Nevertheless, he completed three years at the *école*.[[4]](#cite_note-cassell-4) In a letter to his parents, he complained that the life model was a man in the pose of a mediaeval lutanist. During the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") in 1870, Bastien fought and was wounded. After the war, he returned home to paint the villagers and recover from his wound. In 1873 he painted his grandfather in the garden, a work that would bring the artist his first success at the [Paris Salon](/wiki/Paris_Salon "Paris Salon").

### Early work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early work")]

After exhibiting works in the Salons of 1870 and 1872, which attracted no attention, in 1874 his *Portrait of my Grandfather*[[5]](#cite_note-5) garnered critical acclaim and received a third-class medal. He also showed *Song of Spring*, an academically oriented study of rural life, representing a peasant girl sitting on a knoll above a village,[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrantz1911-3) surrounded by wood nymphs.

His initial success was confirmed in 1875 by the *First Communion*, a picture of a little girl minutely worked up in manner that was compared to [Hans Holbein](/wiki/Hans_Holbein_the_Younger "Hans Holbein the Younger"), and a *Portrait of M. Hayern*. In 1875, he took second place in the competition for the *[Prix de Rome](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome "Prix de Rome")* with his *Angels appearing to the Shepherds*, exhibited again at the *Exposition Universelle* in 1878. His next attempt to win the Prix de Rome in 1876 with *Priam at the Feet of Achilles* was again unsuccessful (it is in the [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") gallery), and the painter determined to return to country life.[[Note 1]](#cite_note-6) To the Salon of 1877 he sent a full-length *Portrait of Lady L.* and *My Parents*; and in 1878 a *Portrait of M. Theuriet* and *Haymaking (Les Foins)*.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrantz1911-3) The last picture, now in the [Musée d'Orsay](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_d%27Orsay "Musée d'Orsay"), was widely praised by critics and the public alike. It secured his status as one of the first painters in the Naturalist school.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Jules_Bastien-Lepage_001.jpg/250px-Jules_Bastien-Lepage_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jules_Bastien-Lepage_001.jpg)

*All Souls' Day*, c. 1882

### Naturalism and acclaim

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Naturalism and acclaim")]

After the success of *Haymaking*, Bastien-Lepage was recognized in France as the leader of the emerging Naturalist school. By 1883, a critic could proclaim that "The whole world paints so much today like M. Bastien-Lepage that M. Bastien-Lepage seems to paint like the whole world."[[6]](#cite_note-7) This fame brought him prominent commissions.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/%C2%A7%C2%A7Bernhardt%2C_Sarah_%281844-1923%29_par_Jules_Bastien-Lepage_%281848-1884%29_-_1879.jpg/250px-%C2%A7%C2%A7Bernhardt%2C_Sarah_%281844-1923%29_par_Jules_Bastien-Lepage_%281848-1884%29_-_1879.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C2%A7%C2%A7Bernhardt,_Sarah_(1844-1923)_par_Jules_Bastien-Lepage_(1848-1884)_-_1879.jpg)

*[Portrait of Sarah Bernhardt](/wiki/Portrait_of_Sarah_Bernhardt "Portrait of Sarah Bernhardt")*, 1879

His *[Portrait of Sarah Bernhardt](/wiki/Portrait_of_Sarah_Bernhardt "Portrait of Sarah Bernhardt")* (1879), painted in a light key, won him the cross of the [Legion of Honour](/wiki/Legion_of_Honour "Legion of Honour"). In 1879 he was commissioned to do a portrait of the Prince of Wales. In 1880 he exhibited a small depiction of M. Andrieux and an historical painting of *Joan of Arc* (now in the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art")); and in the same year, at the Royal Academy, the small portrait of the Prince of Wales. In 1881 he painted *The Beggar* and the *Portrait of Albert Wolf*; in 1882 *Le Père Jacques*; in 1885 *Village Love*, in which we find some trace of [Gustave Courbet](/wiki/Gustave_Courbet "Gustave Courbet")'s influence. His last dated work is *The Forge* (1884).[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrantz1911-3)

### Death and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Death and legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Jules_Bastien-Lepage_1884.jpg/250px-Jules_Bastien-Lepage_1884.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jules_Bastien-Lepage_1884.jpg)

Self-portrait drawn a few days before his death

Between 1880 and 1883 he traveled in Italy. The artist, long ailing, had tried in vain to re-establish his health in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers"). He died in Paris in 1884, when planning a new series of rural subjects. His friend, [Prince Bojidar Karageorgevitch](/wiki/Prince_Bojidar_Karageorgevitch "Prince Bojidar Karageorgevitch"),[[7]](#cite_note-8) was with him at the end and wrote:[[4]](#cite_note-cassell-4)

> At last he was unable to work anymore; and he died on the 10th of December, 1884, breathing his last in my arms. At his grave's head his mother and brother planted an apple-tree.

In March and April 1885, more than 200 of his pictures were exhibited at the Ecole des Beaux-Arts. In 1889 some of his best-known work was shown at the [Paris Exposition Universelle](/wiki/Paris_Exposition_Universelle "Paris Exposition Universelle").

Among his more important works, may also be mentioned the portrait of Mme J. Drouet (1883); [Gambetta](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Gambetta "Léon Gambetta") on his death-bed, and some landscapes; *The Vintage* (1880), and *The Thames at London* (1882). *The Little Chimney-Sweep* was never finished.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrantz1911-3) A museum is devoted to him at [Montmédy](/wiki/Montm%C3%A9dy "Montmédy"). A statue of Bastien-Lepage by [Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin") was erected in Damvillers.[[4]](#cite_note-cassell-4) An obituary by Prince Bojidar Karageorgevitch, appeared in the *Magazine of Art* (Cassell) in 1890.[[4]](#cite_note-cassell-4)

### Impact on the reception of Impressionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Impact on the reception of Impressionism")]

The influential English critic [Roger Fry](/wiki/Roger_Fry "Roger Fry") credited the wider public's acceptance of the [Impressionists](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"), especially [Claude Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet"), to Bastien-Lepage. In his 1920 *Essay in Æsthetics*, Fry wrote:[[8]](#cite_note-Fry-9)

> Monet is an artist whose chief claim to recognition lies in the fact of his astonishing power of faithfully reproducing certain aspects of nature, but his really naive innocence and sincerity was taken by the public to be the most audacious humbug, and it required the teaching of men like Bastien-Lepage, who cleverly compromised between the truth and an accepted convention of what things looked like, to bring the world gradually around to admitting truths which a single walk in the country with purely unbiassed vision would have established beyond doubt.

## Relationship with Marie Bashkirtseff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Relationship with Marie Bashkirtseff")]

Ukrainian-born painter [Marie Bashkirtseff](/wiki/Marie_Bashkirtseff "Marie Bashkirtseff") formed a close friendship with Bastien-Lepage.[[9]](#cite_note-10) Artistically, she took her cue from the French painter's admiration for nature: "I say nothing of the fields because Bastien-Lepage reigns over them as a sovereign; but the streets, however, have not still had their...Bastien."[[10]](#cite_note-11) Her best-known work in this naturalist vein is *A Meeting* (now in the Musée d'Orsay), which was shown to wide acclaim at the [Paris Salon](/wiki/Paris_Salon "Paris Salon") of 1884. By a curious coincidence she succumbed to chronic illness the same year as her colleague and friend.

## Art market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Art market")]

The highest price reached by one of his paintings in the art market was when his *Portrait of Sarah Bernhardt* (1879) sold for $2,280,000 at [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's"), on 20 October 2022.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Bastien-Lepage&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Honours")]

* 1883: Knight in the [Order of Leopold](/wiki/Order_of_Leopold_(Belgium) "Order of Leopold (Belgium)").[[13]](#cite_note-14)
